tradeoffs.scenario: An aerospace components manufacturer sources 85% of its titanium forgings from a single supplier with whom it has a deeply integrated quality system, shared production scheduling, and co-developed proprietary alloys. This partnership yields 18% lower defect rates and 22-day lead times versus the industry average of 45 days. However, a recent 3-week supplier disruption cost the company $14M in delayed deliveries and contract penalties.

objective_a.label: Deepen supply chain integration with strategic partners

objective_b.label: Diversify supplier and distribution decisions for resilience

tradeoffs.correct_strategy: Maintain the primary supplier at 60-65% volume with full integration but qualify and develop two secondary suppliers for the remaining 35-40%, sharing enough technical specifications for them to produce acceptable (though not co-developed) alternatives. Invest in dual-qualification testing so secondary sources can scale to 70% within 8 weeks during a disruption.
tradeoffs.correct_sacrifice: Secondary suppliers will have higher defect rates and longer lead times than the primary partner, and maintaining dual-qualified sources requires ongoing qualification costs of approximately $800K-$1.2M annually.
tradeoffs.explanation: The 85% concentration creates catastrophic exposure, as the $14M disruption loss demonstrated. But fully diversifying away from the integrated partner would sacrifice the 18% quality advantage and proprietary alloy access that differentiate the company's products. The 60/35 split preserves the integration benefits that drive quality and speed while creating enough secondary capacity to survive a disruption. The key insight is that secondary suppliers do not need to match the primary's performance — they need to be adequate during emergencies, not optimal during normal operations.

sources.title: From Superstorms to Factory Fires: Managing Unpredictable Supply Chain Disruptions
Risk, Resilience, and Rebalancing in Global Value Chains

distractor_strategies.strategy: Split volume equally across 4 suppliers at 25% each, requiring all suppliers to implement the same integrated quality system and co-develop proprietary alloys to maintain current performance levels
distractor_strategies.sacrifice: No supplier has enough volume to justify the co-development investment, destroying the proprietary alloy advantage and increasing per-unit costs across all sources

distractor_strategies.strategy: Double down on the single supplier by increasing volume to 95% and negotiating a contractual guarantee with $14M penalty clauses for disruptions, using legal protection instead of diversification
distractor_strategies.sacrifice: Penalty clauses do not prevent disruptions or deliver parts — the company still loses production time and customer trust during outages regardless of financial recovery

distractor_strategies.strategy: Build a 90-day strategic inventory buffer of titanium forgings to protect against disruptions while maintaining the single-source relationship unchanged
distractor_strategies.sacrifice: Titanium forgings are high-value items — a 90-day buffer ties up approximately $8-10M in working capital and creates obsolescence risk if specifications change

tradeoffs.scenario: A building materials distributor operates 14 regional warehouses across the eastern United States, averaging 62% utilization. Analysis shows that consolidating to 6 optimally located mega-facilities would reduce fixed operating costs by $11M annually and improve inventory turns from 6.2 to 9.1. However, 40% of customers currently receive same-day delivery, and the consolidated network would extend average delivery time from 4 hours to 14 hours for those customers.

objective_a.label: Optimize facility network for operational efficiency

objective_b.label: Maintain demand proximity and service levels

tradeoffs.correct_strategy: Consolidate to 8 facilities (not 6) by closing the 6 lowest-utilization warehouses, while converting 4-5 of the closed locations into lean forward-staging points that hold only the top 50 high-velocity SKUs for same-day delivery. The staging points operate at minimal cost with small footprints while preserving same-day capability for the products that drive 80% of urgent orders.
tradeoffs.correct_sacrifice: Same-day delivery for low-velocity, specialty products is lost — those items ship from mega-facilities with next-day delivery. The staging points add $1.5-2M in operating costs, reducing the net savings from $11M to approximately $7-8M.
tradeoffs.explanation: The full consolidation to 6 facilities optimizes for cost but ignores that same-day delivery is a competitive differentiator in building materials, where contractors face project delays from material shortages. The hybrid approach recognizes that not all products need same-day proximity — Pareto analysis of urgent orders reveals that a small subset of high-velocity SKUs drives most same-day demand. Forward-staging those items preserves the service level that matters while capturing most of the consolidation savings from reduced fixed costs and improved inventory turns.

sources.title: Network Optimization in Supply Chain Design

distractor_strategies.strategy: Consolidate fully to 6 mega-facilities as analyzed and compensate for lost same-day delivery by offering free next-day shipping on all orders, absorbing the expedited freight costs
distractor_strategies.sacrifice: Expedited freight for 40% of customers who previously received same-day delivery could cost $4-6M annually, eroding most of the $11M savings while still degrading service levels

distractor_strategies.strategy: Keep all 14 warehouses open but reduce inventory at each location to only high-velocity items, centralizing slow-moving stock at 3 regional hubs to improve overall inventory turns
distractor_strategies.sacrifice: 14 facilities at 62% utilization still carry the full fixed cost burden — the strategy improves turns but captures none of the $11M facility consolidation savings

distractor_strategies.strategy: Consolidate to 6 facilities and partner with a same-day courier service to maintain the 4-hour delivery window for all products from the consolidated locations
distractor_strategies.sacrifice: Same-day courier delivery of bulk building materials over extended distances is prohibitively expensive and logistically impractical for heavy, oversized items

tradeoffs.scenario: An industrial equipment manufacturer under pressure from activist investors needs to improve EBITDA margins from 8% to 12% within 18 months. The CFO proposes cutting supply chain spending by 15% ($22M), targeting warehousing, supplier development programs, and IT systems upgrades. However, the company's 5-year strategic plan depends on launching a connected-equipment platform that requires IoT-enabled supply chain capabilities not yet built.

objective_b.label: Invest in supply chain capabilities that create long-term company value

tradeoffs.correct_strategy: Categorize the $22M spending into three tiers: (1) cut $9M in non-strategic costs through warehouse consolidation and logistics renegotiation, (2) defer $5M in nice-to-have system upgrades by 12-18 months, and (3) protect $8M in supplier development and IoT infrastructure investments that directly enable the connected-equipment platform. Present investors with the $14M in immediate savings plus a funded roadmap showing how the protected investments generate 3x returns within 3 years.
tradeoffs.correct_sacrifice: The margin improvement reaches approximately 10.5% in 18 months rather than the full 12% target, requiring the company to demonstrate the remaining gap will close as platform revenue materializes.
tradeoffs.explanation: Activist investors respond to credible plans that show both discipline and growth — pure cost cutting signals decline, while ignoring margin pressure risks a proxy fight. The tiered approach demonstrates expense management rigor (cutting $14M proves seriousness) while protecting investments tied to a quantifiable growth strategy. The connected-equipment platform represents the kind of supply chain value creation that transforms cost centers into revenue enablers. By linking the protected $8M to specific revenue milestones, the company reframes supply chain investment from expense to strategic asset.

sources.title: The Case for Investing More in Your Supply Chain
Supply Chain as a Source of Competitive Advantage

distractor_strategies.strategy: Implement the full $22M cost reduction as proposed by the CFO to hit the 12% EBITDA target, then request a separate capital expenditure budget for the IoT platform in the following fiscal year
distractor_strategies.sacrifice: Cutting supplier development and IT upgrades now destroys the foundation needed for the IoT platform — rebuilding those capabilities later costs 2-3x more than maintaining them

distractor_strategies.strategy: Reject the cost-cutting mandate entirely and instead present investors with a 5-year value creation thesis showing how increased supply chain investment will generate superior long-term returns
distractor_strategies.sacrifice: Activist investors operating on 18-month timelines will likely launch a proxy fight, potentially replacing management before the long-term strategy can prove itself

distractor_strategies.strategy: Outsource the entire supply chain operation to a 3PL/4PL partner, converting fixed costs to variable costs to immediately improve EBITDA margins while transferring IoT development responsibility to the outsourcing partner
distractor_strategies.sacrifice: Outsourcing transfers control of strategic supply chain capabilities to a third party whose incentives may not align with the connected-equipment platform vision
